vulnerability.title: Set number of Password Hashing Rounds - password-auth
vulnerability.description: Configure the number or rounds for the password hashing algorithm. This can be accomplished by using the rounds option for the pam_unix PAM module. In file /etc/pam.d/password-auth append rounds= to the pam_unix.so entry, as shown below: password sufficient pam_unix.so ...existing_options... rounds= The system's default number of rounds is 5000.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( rpm --quiet -q pam && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ); then var_password_pam_unix_rounds='' if [ -e "/etc/pam.d/password-auth" ] ; then PAM_FILE_PATH="/etc/pam.d/password-auth" if [ -f /usr/bin/authselect ]; then if ! authselect check; then echo " authselect integrity check failed. Remediation aborted! This remediation could not be applied because an authselect profile was not selected or the selected profile is not intact. It is not recommended to manually edit the PAM files when authselect tool is available. In cases where the default authselect profile does not cover a specific demand, a custom authselect profile is recommended." exit 1 fi CURRENT_PROFILE=$(authselect current -r | awk '{ print $1 }') # If not already in use, a cust

triage_decision.reason: Adding the rounds option to pam_unix is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect service availability or user authentication beyond normal password hashing; it follows the recommended pattern and is unlikely to cause lockout.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect syntax could prevent PAM from loading, potentially locking out local users

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly adds the rounds=5000 option to the pam_unix entry in /etc/pam.d/password-auth, which meets the vulnerability requirement. It includes safety checks for authselect integrity and avoids duplicate options. While the approach will resolve the issue, there are minor improvements that can be made to enhance robustness and maintainability.
concerns: No backup of the original PAM file is performed, so recovery could be more difficult if an unexpected error occurs.
The logic currently targets only the first non-commented pam_unix.so line; if multiple pam_unix entries exist, some may remain unchanged.
If the rounds option is present with a non-default value, the script does not modify it; this could leave the system with inadequate rounds.
The plan relies on sed which may modify the line globally; precise matching would be safer.
The script does not explicitly handle the case where the rounded value is already set but not as 5000, which might be a configuration drift.
No explicit testing of the change against a PAM-specific test harness is performed.
The plan does not provide a rollback mechanism if the change causes authentication issues.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup copy of /etc/pam.d/password-auth before editing.
Add logic to detect and handle multiple pam_unix.so lines, ensuring all are updated or at least documenting the decision.
If a rounds option exists but differs from 5000, decide whether to replace it or leave as is – consider using a warning or policy statement for non-default values.
Use more precise sed or awk patterns to avoid accidental modification of other options or commented lines.
Include a pre-change and post-change authentication test, such as using pamtester, to validate that authentication still succeeds.
Provide a rollback or revert script that restores the original PAM file from the backup if needed.
Document the change in an audit log or runlog for compliance tracking.
